content:
# Ruth Chapter 2 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 880-881 | **Source Image:** RUT_002_page_001.jpg, RUT_002_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now Naomi had a kinsman of her husband's, a man of great worth, from the family of Elimelech, and his name was Boaz.

**2** And Ruth the Moabitess said to Naomi, "Let me go, I pray, to the field, and let me glean among the ears of grain after him in whose eyes I may find favor." And she said to her, "Go, my daughter."

**3** So she went and came and gleaned in the field after the reapers. And as it happened, the portion of the field belonged to Boaz, who was of the family of Elimelech.

**4** And behold, Boaz came from Bethlehem and said to the reapers, "The LORD be with you." And they said to him, "The LORD bless you."

**5** And Boaz said to his servant who was set over the reapers, "Whose young woman is this?"

**6** And the servant who was set over the reapers answered and said, "She is a young Moabite woman who returned with Naomi from the fields of Moab.

**7** She said, 'Let me glean, I pray, and gather among the sheaves after the reapers.' She came and has remained from the morning until now; she has rested in the shelter but little."

**8** And Boaz said to Ruth, "Have you not heard, my daughter? Do not go to glean in another field, and do not pass on from here, but stay close to my young women.

**9** Let your eyes be on the field which they are reaping, and go after them. Have I not commanded the young men not to touch you? And when you are thirsty, go to the vessels and drink from what the young men have drawn."

**10** And she fell upon her face and bowed down to the ground, and she said to him, "Why have I found favor in your eyes, that you should take notice of me, seeing that I am a foreigner?"

**11** And Boaz answered and said to her, "It has been fully reported to me, all that you have done for your mother-in-law since the death of your husband -- how you left your father and your mother and the land of your birth, and came to a people whom you did not know before.

**12** May the LORD repay your work, and may your wages be full from the LORD, the God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take refuge."

**13** And she said, "May I find favor in your eyes, my lord, for you have comforted me, and you have spoken to the heart of your maidservant, though I am not even as one of your maidservants."

**14** And Boaz said to her at mealtime, "Come here and eat of the bread, and dip your morsel in the vinegar." And she sat beside the reapers, and he held out to her roasted grain. And she ate and was satisfied and had some left over.

**15** And when she rose to glean, Boaz commanded his young men, saying, "Let her glean even among the sheaves, and do not put her to shame.

**16** And also pull out some from the bundles for her and leave them, that she may glean, and do not rebuke her."

**17** So she gleaned in the field until evening. And she beat out what she had gleaned, and it was about an ephah of barley.

**18** And she carried it and came into the city, and her mother-in-law saw what she had gleaned. And she brought out and gave to her what she had left over after she was satisfied.

**19** And her mother-in-law said to her, "Where did you glean today, and where did you work? May the one who took notice of you be blessed." And she told her mother-in-law with whom she had worked, and said, "The name of the man with whom I worked today is Boaz."

**20** And Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, "Blessed be he of the LORD, who has not forsaken his faithful love toward the living and toward the dead." And Naomi said to her, "The man is a near kinsman to us; he is one of our kinsman-redeemers."

**21** And Ruth the Moabitess said, "He also said to me, 'You shall stay close to my young men until they have finished all my harvest.'"

**22** And Naomi said to Ruth, her daughter-in-law, "It is good, my daughter, that you go out with his young women, so that they do not meet you in another field."

**23** And she stayed close to the young women of Boaz to glean until the end of the barley harvest and the wheat harvest. And she dwelt with her mother-in-law.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The majority of Chapter 2's verses showed strong agreement across all three translators in substance. Verses 4, 9, 14, 17, 18, 19, 22, and 23 required only minor stylistic arbitration. SOPHIA followed the formal-equivalent center of gravity throughout.

Manuscript verification: The Leningrad Codex images confirm the continuation of Ruth on pages 880-881. The text flows seamlessly from Chapter 1, with no chapter division visible in the manuscript (chapter divisions being a later convention). The Masoretic notation is visible in the margins.

Key term decisions for this chapter:
- **gibbor hayil** (2:1): Rendered "man of great worth" (LOGOS) -- capturing both social standing and character.
- **hesed** (2:20): Rendered "faithful love" -- consistent with the Chapter 1 decision.
- **go'el** (2:20): Rendered "kinsman-redeemer" -- preserving the full legal-theological term.
- **kenafav** (2:12): Rendered "wings" -- all three agree; the divine protection imagery foreshadows 3:9.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 -- "kinsman" vs. "relative" and "mighty of valor" vs. "great worth" vs. "considerable standing"
- **EMET:** "a-kinsman of-her-husband, a-man mighty of-valor"
- **LOGOS:** "a kinsman of her husband's, a man of great worth"
- **PNEUMA:** "a relative on her husband's side, a man of considerable standing"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Kinsman" preserves the Hebrew meyudda' (one known to the family) better than PNEUMA's generic "relative." "Great worth" for gibbor hayil captures both material and moral weight, while EMET's "mighty of valor" is too martial and PNEUMA's "considerable standing" too socially narrow.

### Verse 2 -- "let me glean among the ears of grain" vs. "gather leftover grain behind anyone who will allow me"
- **EMET:** "let-me-glean among-the-ears-of-grain after [him] in-whose eyes I-find favor"
- **LOGOS:** "let me glean among the ears of grain after him in whose eyes I may find favor"
- **PNEUMA:** "Let me go out to the fields and gather leftover grain behind anyone who will allow me"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's rendering loses the specific Hebrew phrase bashibbolim ("among the ears of grain") and restructures the clause significantly. The Hebrew's deference ("in whose eyes I may find favor") is theologically important.

### Verse 3 -- "her chance happened" vs. "it happened that" vs. "As it happened"
- **EMET:** "And-her-chance happened [upon] a-portion of-the-field"
- **LOGOS:** "And it happened that the portion of the field belonged to Boaz"
- **PNEUMA:** "As it happened, she found herself in the portion of field belonging to Boaz"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA combines elements: "And as it happened, the portion of the field belonged to Boaz." The Hebrew vayyiqer miqreha is a deliberate narrative device (the narrator's theological wink at providence). EMET's literal "her-chance happened" is too wooden, but the element of apparent coincidence should be preserved. SOPHIA's "as it happened" nods to this subtlety.

### Verse 5 -- "To whom belongs this young woman?" vs. "Whose young woman is this?" vs. "Who is that young woman?"
- **EMET:** "To-whom [belongs] this young-woman?"
- **LOGOS:** "Whose young woman is this?"
- **PNEUMA:** "Who is that young woman?"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew lemi ("to whom") asks about social belonging, not identity. PNEUMA's "Who is that" loses this dimension.

### Verse 6 -- "field of Moab" vs. "fields of Moab" vs. "country of Moab"
- **EMET:** "the-[one]-returning with Naomi from-[the]-field of-Moab"
- **LOGOS:** "who returned with Naomi from the fields of Moab"
- **PNEUMA:** "who came back with Naomi from the country of Moab"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "fields of Moab." The Hebrew here uses the singular sedeh but SOPHIA maintains consistency with the plural used elsewhere in the book. PNEUMA's "country" is an interpretive departure.

### Verse 7 -- Difficult final clause
- **EMET:** "this [is] her-sitting the-house a-little"
- **LOGOS:** "she has rested in the shelter but little"
- **PNEUMA:** "with barely a moment's rest in the shelter"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. This clause is notoriously difficult (as EMET's note acknowledges). EMET preserves the difficulty but produces unintelligible English. LOGOS and PNEUMA both interpret it as brief rest; LOGOS is more formal. PNEUMA's "barely a moment's rest" adds interpretive coloring.

### Verse 8 -- "stay close" / "cling" / "stay close"
- **EMET:** "thus you-shall-cling [tidbaqin] to my-young-women"
- **LOGOS:** "but stay close to my young women"
- **PNEUMA:** "Stay close to my young women"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "stay close." While EMET correctly identifies the verb as davaq (the same "cling" as 1:14), rendering it "cling" in this context would sound unnatural in English. The verbal echo is noted in the council notes rather than forced into the translation.

### Verse 10 -- "seeing that I am a foreigner" vs. "though I am" vs. "I -- a foreigner?"
- **EMET:** "and-I [am] a-foreigner?"
- **LOGOS:** "seeing that I am a foreigner?"
- **PNEUMA:** "Why would you take notice of me -- a foreigner?"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "seeing that I am a foreigner" -- the subordinate clause properly renders the Hebrew circumstantial clause. PNEUMA restructures the entire question.

### Verse 11 -- "yesterday and the third day" vs. "before" vs. "before"
- **EMET:** "a-people which you-did-not know yesterday [and the]-third-[day]"
- **LOGOS:** "a people whom you did not know before"
- **PNEUMA:** "a people you did not know before"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew idiom temol shilshom ("yesterday and the day before") means "previously" or "before." EMET's literal rendering is an English misfire for this standard idiom. LOGOS and PNEUMA both correctly render the sense.

### Verse 11 -- Infinitive absolute emphasis
- **EMET:** "It-has-been-told, it-was-told to-me"
- **LOGOS:** "It has been fully reported to me"
- **PNEUMA:** "I have been told everything"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew infinitive absolute (hugged huggad) creates emphasis. LOGOS's "fully reported" captures the emphasis elegantly. PNEUMA loses the construction entirely.

### Verse 12 -- "repay your deed" / "repay your work" / "repay you fully"
- **EMET:** "May-YHWH-repay your-deed"
- **LOGOS:** "May the LORD repay your work"
- **PNEUMA:** "May the LORD repay you fully for what you have done"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew po'alekh means "your work/deed." PNEUMA restructures the sentence and adds "for what you have done."

### Verse 12 -- "take refuge" vs. "take shelter"
- **EMET:** "to-take-refuge under His-wings"
- **LOGOS:** "under whose wings you have come to take refuge"
- **PNEUMA:** "under whose wings you have come to take shelter"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "take refuge" -- the Hebrew lahasot (from hasah) specifically means to seek refuge, and "refuge" is the standard theological rendering.

### Verse 13 -- "maidservant" vs. "servant"
- **EMET:** "your-maidservant... I-am not as-one of-your-maidservants"
- **LOGOS:** "your maidservant, though I am not even as one of your maidservants"
- **PNEUMA:** "your servant -- though I am not even one of your servant girls"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "maidservant" -- the Hebrew shifhah is specifically a female servant, and "maidservant" preserves this distinction. PNEUMA's "servant girls" is colloquial.

### Verse 15 -- "do not put her to shame" vs. "do not reproach her" vs. "don't humiliate her"
- **EMET:** "do-not-put-her-to-shame"
- **LOGOS:** "do not reproach her"
- **PNEUMA:** "don't humiliate her"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET with "do not put her to shame" -- the Hebrew takhliymuha (from kalam) means to shame or humiliate. "Put to shame" is the most precise formal rendering. LOGOS's "reproach" shifts the sense toward verbal criticism.

### Verse 16 -- "pulling you shall pull out" vs. "pull out some" vs. "pull out some stalks on purpose"
- **EMET:** "pulling you-shall-pull-out for-her from the-bundles"
- **LOGOS:** "pull out some from the bundles for her"
- **PNEUMA:** "pull out some stalks from the bundles on purpose and leave them for her to pick up"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. EMET's infinitive absolute rendering is awkward. PNEUMA's "on purpose" and "for her to pick up" are interpretive additions, though the intent is correct.

### Verse 20 -- hesed: "lovingkindness" vs. "faithful love" vs. "loyal love"
- **EMET:** "his-lovingkindness [hesed]"
- **LOGOS:** "His faithful love"
- **PNEUMA:** "his loyal love"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "faithful love" -- consistent with the Chapter 1 rendering. See Chapter 1 disagreement log for full rationale.

### Verse 20 -- "has not abandoned" vs. "has not forsaken" vs. "has not abandoned"
- **EMET:** "who has-not-abandoned his-lovingkindness"
- **LOGOS:** "who has not forsaken His faithful love"
- **PNEUMA:** "He has not abandoned his loyal love"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "forsaken" -- the Hebrew 'azav carries a stronger, more covenantal sense than "abandoned" and "forsaken" preserves the theological gravity.

### Verse 20 -- go'el: "kinsman-redeemers" vs. "kinsman-redeemer" vs. "family redeemers"
- **EMET:** "from-our-kinsman-redeemers [go'alenu] he [is]"
- **LOGOS:** "he is one of our kinsman-redeemers"
- **PNEUMA:** "He is one of our family redeemers"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "kinsman-redeemers." The compound term preserves both the kinship and redemption dimensions of go'el. PNEUMA's "family redeemers" loses the traditional theological resonance.

### Verse 21 -- "young men" (masculine) preserved
- **EMET:** "With the-young-men who [are] to-me you-shall-cling"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall stay close to my young men"
- **PNEUMA:** "Stay close to my workers"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "young men" -- the Hebrew hanne'arim is masculine, and this is significant: Ruth reports Boaz's words using the masculine term, while Naomi in v. 22 corrects to "young women" (na'arotav). This subtle narrative detail, preserved by EMET and LOGOS, is lost in PNEUMA's gender-neutral "workers."

---

*Signed, SOPHIA*
